prospects.thesis: Edison International is a Southern California regulated utility benefiting from surging AI/data center power demand and California's grid modernization capex cycle. Bullish options and institutional flow alongside a reasonable 1.5x P/S suggest smart money is positioning ahead of continued rate base growth, while utilities remain a defensive play against the sticky 4.58% 10Y yield backdrop. Wildfire liability overhangs have eased, providing a cleaner narrative into H2 2026.

Veeva is the dominant cloud software platform for life sciences (CRM, clinical, quality) and is monetizing AI-native workflows across pharma customers migrating off Salesforce's Veeva partnership. The 19.9% one-month rally with bullish options and institutional support signals accelerating conviction into the next earnings print. Sticky enterprise SaaS revenues justify the 9.6x P/S given secular pharma R&D digitization tailwinds.

GitLab's Duo AI-assisted DevSecOps platform is gaining enterprise traction as a unified alternative to GitHub Copilot, with premium tier attach rates driving ARR expansion. Bullish options and institutional flow with a +14.6% one-month move suggest accumulation ahead of AI code-gen adoption inflection. At 5.4x P/S with a $5.4B cap, it's a more reasonably valued AI infrastructure play than mega-cap peers.

RTX offers diversified exposure across Raytheon missile defense, Pratt & Whitney engines, and Collins Aerospace amid a global rearmament cycle and record DoD budgets. Bullish institutional positioning and the GTF engine remediation moving past peak headwinds set up a multi-year FCF re-rating story. At $260B cap with 2.9x P/S, it's a lower-beta way to play defense tech versus small-cap peers, complementing the watchlist's existing exposure.

PacBio's long-read sequencing (Revio, Vega) platforms are gaining share in clinical genomics and pharma research as HiFi accuracy becomes table stakes for rare disease and oncology workflows. The +13% one-month move on bullish options/institutional flow signals a potential turnaround from prior instrument demand softness. However, at just $0.5B cap with ongoing cash burn, size it small — this is a higher-risk asymmetric bet on sequencing recovery.

market_pulse.rotation_analysis: Big money is piling into the classic "rates are coming down" playbook — Real Estate is lighting up with 3.5x normal call volume, and Utilities, Financials, and Energy are all flashing bullish, which tells me institutions are positioning for a Fed that finally cuts and a Treasury that keeps issuing debt to fund elevated deficit spending. Real Estate in particular smells like a bet that the recent housing affordability push in Washington plus lower mortgage rates unlocks a REIT rally, while Energy is catching a bid from continued tariff tensions, Middle East risk premium, and a permitting environment that's friendlier to domestic producers under the current administration. On the flip side, the heavy put buying in Industrials (an eye-popping 11:1 put/call), Consumer Staples, and Tech looks like smart money hedging against tariff blowback — Industrials get hit both ways from retaliatory tariffs and slowing global capex, and the Tech hedging likely reflects nerves around AI antitrust chatter, China export controls, and stretched valuations heading into earnings. The contradiction worth noting: Consumer Staples supposedly being defensive, yet showing a 6:1 put/call ratio with unusual volume — that's a tell that big players think even "safe" consumer names get squeezed if tariffs push input costs up while strapped consumers trade down, and it lines up with the bearish Consumer Discretionary flow. Widening credit spreads plus that bizarre 32 P/C on investment-grade bonds (LQD) is the quiet scream nobody's covering — institutions are paying up for crash insurance on high-quality corporate debt, which historically precedes either a growth scare or a credit event within 6-8 weeks. The non-obvious read: this isn't a "risk-off" rotation, it's a "stag
